We're introducing Codex Security.

An application security agent that helps you secure your codebase by finding vulnerabilities, validating them, and proposing fixes you can review and patch.

Now, teams can focus on the vulnerabilities that matter and ship code faster.

Codex Security began as Aardvark, launched last year in private beta.

Since then, we’ve significantly improved signal quality, reducing noise, improving severity accuracy, and lowering false positives, so findings better align with real-world risk.
Codex Security is rolling out as a research preview to ChatGPT Enterprise, Business, and Edu customers via Codex web, with free usage for the next month.

We partnered with Mozilla to test Claude's ability to find security vulnerabilities in Firefox.

Opus 4.6 found 22 vulnerabilities in just two weeks. Of these, 14 were high-severity, representing a fifth of all high-severity bugs Mozilla remediated in 2025.

Frontier models are now world-class vulnerability researchers, but they’re currently better at finding vulnerabilities than exploiting them.

This is unlikely to last. We urge developers to redouble their efforts to make software more secure.

there is still no substitute for perfectly understanding every single line of code in your codebase

i fall into the trap of just skimming through ai changes to "just make sure it looks good" all the time, and it makes me lose so much time to not perfectly understand every line

sometimes i feel like we act like this is not true, but everytime we do we just slow ourselves down

now the good part is that you can use ai to understand every line of code 10x faster
as long as we have to read code this is true, there is nothing inbetween "having to perfectly understand every single line" and "never reading through any code". it's just two absolute states

people that do cold outreach ALWAYS ask for favors, like "i need a mentor/job/advice"
 
giving the recipient a single reason to care puts you in top 10 cold messages they have ever received. send them some free work, or if that's impossible say what you can do for them

- here is a video of a feature i added to your app, will make it better because x y z
- i just used your product a lot, here is a list of things i'd change and why
- i read your blog post, i like it but <insert genuine novel thought>

this is not some moral statement of what's good and bad, respectful or not

simply that in this situation both you and the person you message think 100% only of yourselves, and that you can both benefit by you thinking hard how you can help that other person
in my culture we don't say "I love you". we say "I left a comment on your PR that's a prompt you can just send straight to Claude Code"

Balanced brackets dev challenge. Three types with one goal: the shortest correct solution.

Can you write a function that validates (), [], and {} without breaking on edge cases?

A correct solution needs a stack. Every opening bracket is pushed onto the stack. Every closing bracket must match the most recent opening bracket.

If a mismatch occurs, or if the stack is not empty at the end, the string is not balanced. The core idea is validating structure
